nativeRange.habitat: Shallow to mid-depth reef environments including back reef slopes, lagoons, outer reef channels, and subtidal rocky substrates; thrives in areas with moderate light and water movement

decorPreferences: open spacing from neighboring corals (minimum 10–15 cm clearance)
indirect moderate flow — avoid direct powerhead blasting

diet.notes: Primarily photosynthetic via zooxanthellae (Symbiodinium). Supplemental target-feeding one to two times per week with small meaty foods such as mysis shrimp, brine shrimp, reef roids, copepods, or coral-specific blended foods tends to support growth and coloration. Feeding is generally best done at night when polyps and tentacles are extended. Corals that appear healthy in a well-lit, stable system can persist on photosynthesis alone, but occasional feeding is broadly recommended by hobbyists.

goodWith: non-aggressive LPS corals with adequate spacing
avoidWith: other corals placed within sweeper tentacle reach (5–10 cm)
aggressive LPS corals (Galaxea, Hammer, Torch) without generous spacing
fish known to nip at LPS polyps (some butterflyfish)

summary: The Platygyra Brain Coral is a large polyp stony coral prized for its distinctive maze-like ridge-and-valley patterning. Generally considered a beginner-to-intermediate species, it tends to do well in moderate lighting and flow within stable reef water chemistry.
careNotes:
Platygyra is a genus of approximately 11 recognized species. The trade primarily sells P. daedalea, the most common and widespread species, though P. lamellina, P. sinensis, and others are occasionally encountered.

Platygyra corals are prized for their continuous, winding ridge-and-valley patterning — a growth form called meandroid, meaning the skeleton forms long, curving channels rather than individual cups — which gives colonies a strong resemblance to the folded surface of a mammalian brain. The raised ridges (called septa) are toothed and protuberant, and a central ridge called the columella runs along the floor of each channel. Colonies form massive domed or flattened encrusting heads and have been reported to reach a metre or more across in the wild, though aquarium specimens more commonly reach 15–40 cm.

New specimens should be acclimated gradually — starting at the lower end of the tank's light intensity and adjusting over 2–4 weeks — as bleaching can occur from abrupt light increases. LED and T5 systems work well; blue-spectrum emphasis is commonly preferred.
• Flow: Moderate, indirect, random-pattern flow is generally recommended. Flow sufficient to carry away detritus and deliver food is important; direct powerhead blast on the colony tends to cause tissue stress.
• Placement: Lower to middle rockwork or a stable sandbed is typical. Ensure at least 10–15 cm of clear space from neighboring corals to prevent sweeper tentacle contact.
• Aggression: Platygyra extend sweeper tentacles — generally reported in the 5–10 cm range — particularly at night and when food is detected. These tentacles can sting and damage neighboring corals. Planning adequate spacing from all neighbors is the safe approach.
• Water chemistry: Platygyra consumes meaningful calcium during growth. Stable alkalinity (8–10 dKH), calcium (390–450 ppm), and moderate magnesium (1250–1350 ppm) are broadly recommended. Nitrate up to ~20 ppm is tolerated but lower levels are preferred; phosphate should also be kept low.
• Color morphs: Platygyra are available in a wide range of colors — greens, purples, tans, grays, and browns — often with contrasting valley and ridge coloration. Vivid 'alien' or 'tyree' morphs tend to command premium prices in the frag trade.
• Bleaching and disease: Platygyra tends to be susceptible to bleaching and tissue recession if water quality or lighting conditions decline. Standard LPS pest protocols (dipping and quarantine) are advisable when acquiring new specimens.
breedingNotes: Fragging is possible but tends to be slower and more challenging than with branching LPS — Platygyra are massive corals, and frag cuts must preserve full tissue coverage over skeleton to succeed. Sexual reproduction in the wild occurs via broadcast spawning; captive sexual breeding is not practiced in the hobby. Frag plugs and rubble bases are the typical propagation method in the trade.

primary.name: LiveAquaria – LPS Hard Corals
primary.notes: LiveAquaria Platygyra species-specific page returned 404; the slice primary source was used for context. Primary husbandry data sourced from ReefChasers and ExtreCorals care guides due to LiveAquaria page unavailability.

additional.name: ReefChasers – Platygyra Coral Care Guide
additional.notes: Used as effective primary source. Provided temperature (77–84°F / 25–29°C), pH 8.0–8.4, alkalinity 8–9 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, PAR 75–125, flow moderate, care level beginner, placement middle/lower.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – How to Care for Platygyra in a Reef Tank
additional.notes: Secondary cross-check. Temperature 76–80°F (24–27°C), pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.024–1.026, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, PAR 150–250 (higher than other sources — averaged down), flow moderate, care level beginner-to-intermediate, tank minimum 30 gallons.

additional.name: Extreme Corals – Care Requirements of Platygyra in Home Reef Aquariums
additional.notes: Additional cross-check. Confirmed PAR 80–150, magnesium 1250–1350 ppm, alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 400–450 ppm, salinity 1.024–1.026, placement lower-to-middle, care level beginner-to-intermediate, feeding 1–2× weekly.

additional.name: Salty Underground – Maze Brain Coral Care
additional.notes: Cross-check for aggression and alkalinity. Noted alkalinity 8–10 dKH, calcium 380–420 ppm, temperature 77–79°F (25–26°C), salinity 1.024–1.026, moderate care level, sweeper tentacles described as generally short.

additional.name: Wikipedia – Platygyra daedalea (taxonomy, morphology, native range, depth)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Merulinidae / Scleractinia), native range (Madagascar, East Africa, Red Sea, Gulf of Aden, to Australia, Indonesia, Japan, South China Sea), depth range (subtidal to ~30 m), and colony size (up to ~1 m diameter in the wild). Not used for husbandry parameters.
